body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

controls to actually start standardizing controls um one way in which the old way is is just that we had uh you know various entities hyperscalers and other types um deploying stuff globally without getting you know properly written up which is which has some advantages for everybody so they're they're different I mean we we'll see there's going to be rechartering here pretty soon um we'll see what exactly decide to adopt uh you can see see some candidates there many of you've probably heard of bbr if you're at all adjacent to the space um and also like it that group is certainly open for BCPS other sort of general advice we'll see what comes in that space and what people want to do mask uh there's some mask Enthusiast in room I believe um so uh quick has uh something in it called datagrams so quick is basically TCP service guarantees very at the very highest level um so it doesn't have unreliable things natively but there's this extension called datagrams and that allows you to provide UDP like uh service guarantees to application traffic and so the basic deal with mask is you're taking htb connect which most of you probably familiar with um and extending it Beyond TCP to carry other things mostly in datagram so UDP which is done um and by extension quick over UDP uh IP which is done uh the listen side of UDP which is not done um quick but skipping some of the overhead of actually explicitly having UDP and finally and most recently and hot off the press is ethernet so uh many people like to joke that we're then going to go and do like 5G and in carrier pigeon and whatever else over HV connect I hope I'm not giving up the plot but uh in theory this is one day gon to end once we've once we've done everything we need to do okay so so why just some use cases for this one is uh privacy proxy where um you have a client connected to Target and it wants to obscure uh its its identity um it opens a a hp3 connection HTP connect con excuse me hp3 connection using connect could be another version of HTTP but uh that's a bad idea um and then all the uh then there's an ultimate Quick Connect or quick or other UDP connection to a Target that is carried encrypted in the tunnel to the proxy and the proxy kind of decapsulates that and passes it onto the target a second use case is performance- enhancing proxies uh in the transport area when sa performance in haxing proxies you will get a fairly diverse set of opinions and epithets but uh in the TP world uh you split it you can very easily split a connection because no way authenticated and for various extreme length Technologies like satellite or uh certain Mobile use cases uh these are somewhat popular because you can run a very special congestion control for that that very special link type and then do something more normal for the for the connection over the wider internet can't really do that in quick because quick is an authenticated transport um but uh using mask if the client explicitly consents to do it it could connect to the mask uh proxy that outer connection can run a very special congestion control and then the inner uh encapsulated thing would be uh ready for the normal internet and then finally VPN um uh which I mean we all know vpn's are basically you can have two mask proxies on either end of of uh the open internet and then have a a private corporate uh Network or whatever with a bunch of hosts and then just use this proxy to to um to uh tunnel stuff over the Internet that didn't work did I break it okay I've been breaking a lot of these okay nfsv4 um nfsv4 stands for Network file system version four there are three minor versions which I guess are all deployed but uh I don't know for sure because I don't I've never had this working group so they maintain that and remote direct memory access or RDMA um this has this is a pretty insular group that's been doing their thing for a long long time uh they're currently refreshing uh 4.1 they're doing some intern internationalization stuff and uh doing RPC over RDMA uh ask IAD if you have more questions about that because I don't know quick I think you've heard of this uh it's new doesn't really apply anymore it's a reliable transport I already talked about it a little bit um these are the the tldr those like the five thing I would say these are the five reasons we did it um and replace TCP and sctp um uh I'll note extensibility because that's that's like the ITF thing um TCP runs into the very serious problem because of all these middle boxes and all the things that are not encrypted and invisible to the network that there's a lot of enforcement that tries to be helpful that is not actually helpful makes it very very difficult to innovate and so uh from an ITF perspective one of the most exciting things is the extensibility of quick it's all in the encrypted envelope almost all of it and so it's very easy to add new features and and do new Innovative things uh okay so what so that quick has been done for a while what's new um uh they're currently working on standardized logging formats uh I think we all know what TP dump is there there's a large ecosystem of tools based on using packet captures to analyze things like congestion controls that kind of this the congestion control nerds can look at these graphs and understand what's happening um that's that's much harder of course with encrypted packet so uh this logging format called qog creates a a as sounds like a common format to record events and so on and then there's they're building an ecosystem of Tools around that to visualize connections understand what's happening and by the way that's not just a quick thing this this generalized format can be applied to other um uh protocols including hp3 which is being worked on in the quick working group right now uh or the the logging format for it rather and uh we're starting to see a little bit of other working groups um start to build a qog uh formats for their protocols much as you might do for like Yang or something it doesn't all happen to happen doesn't all have to happen in quick um just the normal like protocol performance tweaks that you do anywhere another big thing going on is multipath and there's a little picture of multipath on the right multipath is a weird term uh which means different things in different contexts it used to mean having failover where you have a second path and when the first path goes south you switch to that one quick as had that from the beginning it's called address migration um the other meaning multipath ISM actually sending paths or traffic concurrently on multiple paths in bulk that sounds very appealing it is much trickier than than it sounds to do correctly and uh that is something that that this this work group is working on okay Taps uh as I think someone said is is about to close but the basic idea here is that you know in the old days we had uh like posic sockets API and that was great for doing transport stuff but now um uh well you always had to DNS do DNS resolution separately at the in the application um called directly the application that was not great and now the idea of a transport substrate is a little bit broader I mean I think most hopefully a lot of things go over TLS instead of go over directly over the socket um at the risk of Mark Nottingham um shooting me with a rifle from over there like HTP is often effectively a transport uh for a lot of things um and so and like one one and a lot of the things happen in user space so when you're building applications on top of these other other like upper layer algorithms you end up having to marry that algorithm like you might you know implement it to run on top of op SSL and you know you better hope that op SSL does what you want to do in terms of supporting new features so the idea of TAPS is kind of having a a more generic API that abstracts all of this stuff we had a lot of smart people work on it it's been realized in in Apple's Network framework and that should hopefully make um things more portable between between protocol implementations and also make it easier to innovate by adding new uh by if if you if you implement an application of the transport services API and new protocols come out it should be pre seamless the switch to the new protocol and the new features rather than having to reimplement a whole new API and that should be closing soon all I I already talked about the slide so I'm going to skip all right tcpm I think we all know what TCP is and what TCP maintenance means um as I mentioned earlier extensions are hard because TCP is unprotected um there's still working on some performance tweaks uh like one there really annoying Legacy things about TCP like you can have only 40 bytes of options now that we have things like encrypted cookies in in options that's not really big enough uh and then lastly the tail end of L l4s support which I'll talk about in a moment okay l4s uh so this is something that um the core documents for this were published in tsv WG uh a while um I think a year ago or so um this is a this is the latest flavor of uh explicit congestion notification but the basic idea here is that you have if you use a certain bit in the IP header you go through a different CU with a much shallower buffer and uh the ecn market because you interpret it differently than it it was in the past to say just back off a little bit rather than a lot um and that allows you to sort of fine tune a connection to to operate with very low Q occupancy and you should get much much lower latency across across the uh the path um this is already getting you know this is this is starting to get some legs um the tcpm work here is to is to report the marks to have more Rich reporting of the received marks back to the sender which requires new TCP options and so on on and that work is not quite done even though a lot of the alfors stuff is done and um you know and quick already supported it natively because we knew it was coming uh another thing T oh then there's tsv WG one thing they the a force their for us stuff is almost done but they are responsible for sctp which is a quite old protocol at this point though not as old as TCP um it really doesn't get through middle boxes for the reasons that middle boxes do their thing but it has two use cases that are pretty prevalent number one it is the data channel for for web RTC when it's put over dtls and of course dtls solves the problem that that middleboxes block a protocol number it is also used in service provider signaling networks which of course um don't have some of the middlebox issues that that other places do um so you know like some people sometimes ask me like we have quick now why is SCP still around well that that's why um it may be that that web RTC and other other media options like evolve past this it may eventually as you go from 5G to 6G or some other G like maybe this will this will fall off the table but as it is it's still it's still um it's still very much out there in the internet um and right now there's actually there's an authorization chunk inp and it is broken so we're fixing it uh that seems pretty important and then um there's there's some other like a performance observation who allows you to check some when there's other Integrity protection and then finally dtls overp which is not which is the opposite of SCP over dtls and that is used in um in service provider networks and then uh again tsv WG is kind of the grab bag for transports that don't uh that don't have enough uh traffic to do a have their own working group anymore uh UDP is pretty simple so it it also fits in that category there's a quite intriguing option that is maybe a little that some people might say is too clever called UDP options because there's a UDP header field you Hader link a UDP payload Link Field and an IP Link Field which are kind of redundant and if they're different you could put some stuff in there and that's what that's doing um feel free to send email about that and then dccp is a is a data Grand protocol that that has more has congestion control on like UDP and somebody has a multipath solution of that which I believe has some application in service fider networks and then uh finally some followup to the basic l4s work they already did um operational guidance uh some documents about how to Mark ecn correctly when it's in a tunnel and then a dscp codee point for uh the same CU building behavior that you would otherwise get with our fors anyone have any questions about any of that all right uh enjoy your dinner thanks Martin that was a very good presentation and thanks for your service to the [Applause] tsv okay now we are in the open mic part of the session is there any questions about the new area or are we going to run things or anything related to anything we talked about everything is Crystal Clear I'm waiting if not I think that we can close the session a little bit early so you can have some time back welcome to wit and really glad to see you all here and we'll see you in Vancouver yes welcome to it thank you okay so
